# Part 4 (BT) — The Bluetooth Module {.sdaia-dark background-gradient="linear-gradient(135deg, #1C355E, #00C9A7)"}

## Cutting the Cord

Instead of an infrared remote, we are going to use **Bluetooth**! This means we can control our robot using a smartphone app, just like a real RC car or a drone.

We will use an **HC-05** Bluetooth module and the **Dabble App**, which transforms your smartphone into a virtual controller.


## Wiring the Module

Here is the trick with communication pins: **TX must talk to RX**.

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
1. Connect **VCC** to Arduino `5V`.
2. Connect **GND** to Arduino `GND`.
3. Connect **TXD** to Arduino **Pin 2**.
4. Connect **RXD** to Arduino **Pin 3**.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![](assets/Arduino_BT.png)
:::
::::

::: {.callout-warning .simple}
## The 3.3V Rule
The RXD pin on the HC-05 expects **3.3V**, but the Arduino sends **5V**. For a perfect build, we use a *voltage divider* (two resistors) to lower the voltage. 
:::

## Installing the Dabble Library

Before we can write our code, we need to add the Dabble library to the Arduino IDE:

> 📥 **Download:** [Dabble-Arduino.zip](https://thestempedia.com/download/28488/)

In the Arduino IDE, go to **Sketch** → **Include Library** → **Add .ZIP Library...** and select the file you just downloaded.

![Adding the Library](https://ai.thestempedia.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/Library-Installation.png)

## Connecting with Dabble

To make the Arduino talk to the Dabble app, we just need to include the library and start it in our code. Dabble automatically knows to use Pins 2 and 3!

```cpp
#define CUSTOM_SETTINGS
#define INCLUDE_GAMEPAD_MODULE
#include <Dabble.h>

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(9600); 
  Dabble.begin(9600); // Start Bluetooth communication
}
```

## Install Dabble in your Smartphone

Download the **Dabble App** from your App Store. Once installed, open the app, click the connect icon, and pair it with your HC-05 module (Password is usually `1234` or `0000`).
